3
Essential SVG for D3  
Scalable vector graphics (SVG) is an XML-based markup language for describing two-dimensional graphics.  
It’s the main graphics format used by D3 to create data-driven visualizations.  
SVG shapes are created with XML tags and attributes. !ey are rendered by mathematical equations and  
can be scaled without losing resolution. Individual SVG objects can also be attached to the page’s DOM tree,  
easily manipulated with JavaScript, styled, and transformed with CSS.  
Comprehensive knowledge of SVG is important to make the most of D3, but you only really need to know  
the basics. It’s enough to know how to create simple shapes, such as rectangles, circles, and lines, and how  
to change their attributes and styles. It’s also useful to understand how to apply coordinate transforms, such  
as translation, scaling, and rotation.  
!is chapter provides a tutorial that explores the most important SVG features and advanced SVG DOM  
topics. If you already know SVG, you can safely skip it. If you are new to SVG, it is recommended that you  
practice creating shapes using its main tags and attributes, applying styles and transforms. A"er that, you  
will know enough SVG to start using SVG with D3.  
In this chapter, we’ll cover the following topics:  
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
Creating scalable vector graphics  
Drawing shapes, text, and paths  
Applying #lls, strokes, and opacity  
Inserting images, links, and foreign objects  
A$ne transforms and viewBox  
Group containers and reusable objects  
Using the SVG DOM  
Essential SVG for D3  
2
Technical requirements  
All the code used in this chapter is available in the GitHub repository for this chapter: Chapter03/. !e  
repository contains several subfolders, each containing examples for a certain SVG topic, written in pure  
SVG XML (or SVG DOM) and in D3. You don’t need a web server to run the pure SVG examples and can  
view the results simply by loading the pages directly in your browser.  
Creating scalable vector graphics  
SVG is an open standard. SVG images can be created using a popular vector graphics editor, such as Illustrator,  
Inkscape, or Canva. SVG is also text; you can write SVG markup in any code editor, the same way you write  
HTML code. SVG #les usually have an .svg extension (or .svgz, if compressed) and can be opened in any  
modern browser. You can also load them in an HTML page with the <img> tag. Since SVG graphics are  
vector-based, they are usually much smaller than bitmap images.  
SVG is an XML application. Its elements are part of a namespace usually declared in the root element of an  
SVG #le with the xmlnsattribute and the identi#er http://www.w3.org/2000/svg.  
SVG can also be embedded inside XML and HTML documents. In HTML, adding one or more <svg>elements  
creates a graphics context. When embedded in HTML, you don’t need to declare a namespace. Any valid  
SVG unpre#xed element placed inside an <svg>...</svg>block will be treated as SVG:  
<html>  
<head>...</head>  
<body>  
<svg> <!-- Everything in here belongs to the SVG namespace --> </svg>  
</body>  
</html>  
A namespace is still required in some circumstances. For example, to create SVG elements using a DOM,  
instead of createElement(), you must use createElementNS(), which requires a namespace:  
const h1 = document.createElement("h1"); // create HTML  
const rect =  
document.createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg","rect"); // create SVG  
Without a namespace, the element is still created but won’t be rendered by the browser.  
In most of this book, you never need to declare the SVG namespace, since all SVG graphics will be embedded  
in HTML, and all dynamic element creation will happen using D3, which doesn’t require a namespace in  
most commands (in rare cases, it may require just the svgpre#x).  
Now that you know how SVG is created in an HTML page, let’s look at its most important tags.  
SVG elements  
All SVG elements should be nested as descendants of the <svg>root element – a container that de#nes an  
initial viewport and provides a drawing canvas for shapes and other graphics.  
Creating scalable vector graphics  
3
Table 3.1 lists the main SVG elements and attributes that we will use in this book. !is chapter will provide  
a general introduction to all of them, including code examples that you should try out.  
Tag  
Main attributes  
Description  
<svg>  
height, width, viewBox  
!e root of an SVG. In HTML, it sets a viewport containing all the  
graphics. All elements in this table are children of the <svg>element.  
<circle>  
<rect>  
r, cx, cy  
Draws a circle with radius rand the center at (cx,cy).  
x, y, width, height,  
Draws a rectangle with speci#ed widthand heightvalues and  
the top le" at (x,y). Corner radii can be set with rxand ryfor  
round-corner rectangles.  
rx, ry,  
<line>  
x1, y1, x2, y2  
Draws a line from (x1,y1) to (x2,y2).  
<ellipse>  
Draws an ellipse centered at (cx,cy) with radii rxand ry.  
cx, cy, rx, ry  
<polyline>  
<polygon>  
points,  
Draws an open <polyline>or closed <polygon>connecting the  
provided x,y coordinates. !e fill-rulecan be nonzero(default)  
or evenodd.  
fill-rule  
<path>  
d
Draws an arbitrary path expressed in a compact string containing  
directives to draw lines and curves, set as the d(data) attribute.  
<text>  
x, y,  
Draws text with the baseline and starting point at (x,y). !e baseline  
can be changed with the alignment-baselineproperty and the  
starting point with text-anchor. It can contain <tspan>elements,  
normally placed with relative dxand dycoordinates.  
dx,dy  
<tspan>  
alignment-baseline,  
text-anchor  
<image>  
<g>  
href, x, y  
Inserts an image from a URL in the hrefattribute at position x,y.  
A container. Styles and transforms applied to it a%ect its children.  
Table 3.1 – Most important SVG elements  
!e tags in Table 3.2 are used in some of our projects but are less common in D3 visualizations. Many are  
e%ects that are easier to apply using CSS, while others duplicate functionalities that are easier to implement  
with D3. !ey are brie&y introduced in this chapter.  
Tag  
Main attributes  
Description  
<defs>  
Elements declared inside <defs>are not rendered unless they are  
referenced somewhere outside <defs>.  
<use>  
href  
id  
References any reusable element, marker, or symbol and optionally  
applies new attributes and styles to it.  
<linearGradient>  
<radialGradient>  
A gradient that can be used as a fillor strokecolor for shapes.  
Color stops are de#ned by nested <stop>elements.  
<pattern>  
id  
De#nes reusable patterns applied as a fillor stroke.  
Essential SVG for D3  
4
Tag  
Main attributes  
Description  
<clipPath>  
id, clip-rule  
De#nes a custom clipping path determined by contained shape  
elements. Other elements apply it via the clip-pathattribute.  
<mask>  
id  
id  
De#nes an alpha mask for compositing an object into its  
background. Applied via the maskattribute.  
<filter>  
De#nes a composite #lter created by combining primitives (nested  
<fe...>elements). Elements apply it via the filterattribute.  
<symbol>  
<marker>  
id  
A lightweight reusable graphical template object.  
id, viewBox  
A reusable symbol that can be applied to parts of shapes using the  
marker-start, marker-mid, or marker-endattribute. Typically used  
to draw arrowheads and place objects on vertices.  
<textPath>  
id  
id  
Contains or references a path that can be used as the baseline for  
text. Used to make text &ow on curves.  
<animate>, <set>,  
<animateMotion>,  
<animateTransform>  
Con#gures transitions and animations. Usually applied as children  
to the shape that will be animated.  
Table 3.2 – Other SVG elements that may be occasionally used  
Before drawing some shapes, let’s understand how graphics are rendered in the SVG viewport.  
The initial viewport  
When SVG is embedded in HTML, it creates an initial viewport: a graphics context like the one created  
by Canvas. To create the viewport, all you need is an empty <svg/>tag somewhere in <body>. You can style  
it with standard (HTML) CSS and set its size using widthand heightattributes in pixels, as shown here:  
<style>  
svg { border: solid 1px lightgray;  
background-color: hsla(240,100%,50%,0.2); }  
</style>  
<body><h2>SVG viewport</h2>  
<svg width="600" height="300"></svg>  
</body>  
Drawing basic shapes  
5
Figure 3.1 shows the result of the code (Containers/SVG/1-initial-viewport.html) and an overlay describing  
the default coordinate system used by SVG.  
Figure 3.1 – The SVG viewport embedded in an HTML page. Code: Containers/SVG/1-initial-viewport.html  
!e viewport starts at the top-le" corner of the drawing area, which is 600 pixels wide and 300 pixels high  
(from the height and width attributes). You can change the orientation, scale (units), and several other  
aspects of this coordinate system by con#guring the viewBoxattribute (see the viewBox section).  
Instead of using tags, you can also create and append the SVG root element programmatically to <body>  
with pure JavaScript and the DOM API (see Containers/DOM/1-initial-viewport-dom.html):  
const svg = document.createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg", "svg");  
svg.setAttribute("width", 400);  
svg.setAttribute("height", 300);  
document.getElementsByTagName("body")[0].appendChild(svg);  
In this book, we will either include the <svg/>tag in <body>(or some other HTML container), or create it  
in a <script>block or module using D3 (see Containers/D3/1-initial-viewport.html):  
d3.select("body").append("svg").attr("width", 400).attr("height", 300);  
Now that we have an initial SVG viewport set up, we can start drawing shapes in it.  
Drawing basic shapes  
Shapes are described by XML tags (e.g., <rect>, <circle>, <path>, etc.). SVG graphics are created by declaring  
these tags in an <svg>block and setting required attributes (e.g., radii, points, etc.). Each tag also supports  
attributes that determine its position relative to the local coordinate system.  
!e basic shapes you will probably use most are circles, rectangles, and lines. Let’s start by looking at them.  
Essential SVG for D3  
6
Circles  
You can draw a circle with the <circle> element and the  
r
attribute (radius). If you don’t provide other  
attributes, you will only see the bottom-right quarter of the circle, since its default center is 0,0.  
You can place a circle by providing explicit values for its center via the cx and cy attributes. Unlike CSS  
properties, SVG attributes don’t declare units. Values are expressed in absolute numbers, treated as pixels  
(unless transformed). !e following SVG places di%erent circles in a 200x200-pixel SVG canvas:  
<svg width="200" height="200">  
<circle r="25" />  
<circle cx="40" cy="40" r="20" />  
<circle cx="100" cy="100" r="50" />  
<circle cx="200" cy="200" r="75" />  
</svg>  
!e result of the preceding code is shown in Figure 3.2:  
Figure 3.2 – Drawing circles in the SVG coordinate system. Code: Shapes/SVG/1-shapes.html  
You can obtain an equivalent result using the D OM or D3. !e following code creates the same elements  
and attributes with D3 (see Shapes/D3/1-shapes.html):  
const svg = d3.select("body").append("svg").attr("width", 200).attr("height", 200);  
svg.append("circle").attr("r", 25);  
svg.append("circle").attr("cx", 40).attr("cy", 40).attr("r", 20);  
svg.append("circle").attr("cx", 100).attr("cy", 100).attr("r", 50);  
svg.append("circle").attr("cx", 200).attr("cy", 200).attr("r", 75);  
Of course, the preceding code could be much shorter if we had used D3’s data-binding features or even  
simple loops, but code duplication was maintained so you can compare it to the XML version.  
SVG circles are used in D3 to create scatterplots and bubble charts.  
Rectangles  
A rectangle is created with the <rect>element and requires at least widthand height. !e  
determine its top-le" corner. !e following code draws a cross with two rectangles:  
x
and  
y
attributes  
<svg width="200" height="200">  
<rect x="95" y="10" width="10" height="180" />  
<rect x="10" y="95" width="180" height="10" />  
</svg>  
Drawing basic shapes  
7
Figure 3.3 shows the result:  
Figure 3.3 – Drawing rectangles in the SVG coordinate system. Code: Shapes/SVG/1-shapes.html  
You can create rectangles with rounded corners using the rxand ryattributes, which determine the corner  
radius. In D3, rectangles are used to create classical bar charts.  
Lines  
Lines are created with <line>and require at least four attributes. !e coordinates of the endpoints are provided  
by the x1, y1, x2, and y2attributes. Lines have a default width of 1 pixel, but no default stroke color; they will  
be invisible unless you set one. !e simplest way to do this is via the strokeattribute.  
!e following code draws an X in the middle of the viewport using two lines:  
<svg width="200" height="200">  
<line x1="10" y1="10" x2="190" y2="190" stroke="black"/>  
<line x1="10" y1="190" x2="190" y2="10" stroke="black"/>  
</svg>  
Figure 3.4 shows the result of the preceding code:  
Figure 3.4 – Drawing lines in the SVG coordinate system. Code: Shapes/SVG/2-shapes.html  
!e strokeattribute can be applied to any shape or path. You will learn more about them in the Applying  
styles section.  
Most lines in D3 are created using paths, which are more &exible, but sometimes using <line> is simpler.  
!e same is true about ellipses and polygons.  
Ellipses  
Ellipses have a center at cxand cylike circles, but also two radii: the semi-major and semi-minor axes, set  
with rxand ry. !is code creates a simple ellipse with a horizontal semi-major axis:  
<svg width="200" height="200">  
<ellipse cx="100" cy="100" rx="90" ry="45"/>  
</svg>  
Essential SVG for D3  
8
!e result is shown in Figure 3.5:  
Figure 3.5 – A simple ellipse created with SVG. Code: Shapes/SVG/1-shapes.html  
Ellipses are either tall or wide. If you need an ellipse in another position, you can rotate or shear it using  
transforms. See Shapes/SVG/8-ellipses.htmlfor some examples of transformed ellipses.  
Polygons and polylines  
Polygons (<polygon>) and polylines (<polyline>) render closed or open shapes using straight lines speci#ed  
by a list of space-separated vertex x,y coordinates passed in the pointsattribute. !e only di%erence between  
them is that polygons close the shape automatically, while in polylines, you must repeat the initial coordinates  
to achieve the same result. A fill-rulesets the winding order and determines whether a hole will be drawn  
inside the shape when an outline crosses itself.  
!e following code creates two identical polygons. To shi" the second polygon to the right, we added 200  
to each x coordinate. Since it was created with <polyline>, the #rst coordinates were repeated at the end to  
close the shape. It also declares a fill-rulethat will reveal a hole:  
<svg width="600" height="300">  
<polygon points="150,150 50,150 150,50 200,200 20,200 32,20" fill="blue"/>  
<polyline points="350,150 250,150 350,50 400,200 220,200 232,20 350,150"  
fill="red" fill-rule="evenodd"/>  
</svg>  
!e preceding code was used to draw the shapes shown in Figure 3.6.  
Figure 3.6 – Polygons and polylines created with SVG. Code: Shapes/SVG/9-polygon.html  
Instead of adding an o%set to each coordinate, you can move a shape using transforms. You can even reuse  
the same shape. See examples using these features in Shapes/SVG/9-polygon.html.  
Applying styles  
Shapes can be styled with XML attributes (such as stroke) or CSS properties with the same name, which can  
be applied inline (via the styleattribute) or with a CSS stylesheet.  
Applying styles  
9
Fills and strokes  
Shapes have default black !ll colors and transparent stroke borders, unless you assign di%erent color strings  
via the filland strokeSVG attributes (or CSS properties).  
!e following SVG contains three straight lines. !ey would be invisible if the stroke attribute (or CSS  
property) wasn’t present, since the default stroke color is none. Besides the stroke color, you can also de#ne  
the line’s width with stroke-width, which has the default value of 1:  
<svg width="600" height="200">  
<line x2="400" stroke="red" stroke-width="5"/>  
<line y2="150" stroke="blue" stroke-width="5"/>  
<line x2="200" y2="150" stroke="black"/>  
</svg>  
By default, missing coordinate attributes are zero. In the preceding code, since x1and y1are zero in all lines,  
they all start at the top le". Figure 3.7 shows the result.  
Figure 3.7 – Drawing lines with different stroke colors and stroke widths. Code: Shapes/SVG/2-line-stroke.html  
You can control other stroke attributes (see Shapes/SVG/3-rect-stroke.html and Table 3.3). To outline a  
shape, set its fillproperty to noneor fill-opacityto 0, since shapes have opaque black #lls by default.  
Attribute  
fill  
Description  
!e #ll color of the shape. Default: ‘#000’(black).  
stroke  
!e stroke color of the shape. Default: ‘none’.  
opacity  
!e opacity of the shape. A value from 0 (transparent) to 1 (opaque). Default: 1.  
!e opacity of the #ll color. !is is multiplied by the opacity value.  
!e opacity of the stroke color. !is is multiplied by the opacity value.  
Stroke width, in units (pixels, in a default view box). Default: 1.  
fill-opacity  
stroke-opacity  
stroke-width  
stroke-linecap  
Shape to be used at the ends of open paths or dashes. Can be butt, round, or square.  
Default is butt.  
stroke-dasharray  
Dashing pattern. A sequence of integers alternating a solid line and a space. For  
example, the pattern 5 5will produce 5-unit dashes followed by 5-unit spaces.  
Table 3.3 – Attributes related to fills and strokes  
All XML attributes can also be applied as CSS properties with the same names.  
Essential SVG for D3  
10  
CSS properties  
Style rules can also be set via CSS properties with the same names as the XML attributes. CSS, however,  
has precedence over XML: a style with the same name as an existing attribute will override the attribute.  
Adding the following style declaration in the <style>block of your page will change the width of all lines  
to 20 pixels, ignoring the existing stroke-widthattributes set in each element:  
line { stroke-width: 20px; }  
!is result is shown in Figure 3.8. !e lines at the edges seem thinner because half of their width is outside  
the viewport:  
Figure 3.8 – Overriding XML style attributes with CSS. Code: Shapes/SVG/4-line-css.html  
Inline styling with the styleCSS attribute, due to the cascade rule, overrides any styles previously applied  
via a stylesheet. You can also use the CSS class and id attributes. For example, consider the following  
stylesheet with CSS class declarations:  
.reds  
{ fill: red; }  
.semitr { fill-opacity: 0.5; }  
Since the following rectangles each belong to one or more of these classes, they will inherit the style properties  
declared for each class:  
<svg width="600" height="200">  
<rect x="50" y="50" width="90" height="90" class="semitr"/>  
<rect x="200" y="50" width="175" height="100" rx="40" ry="40" class="reds semitr"/>  
<rect x="450" y="25" width="100" height="150" class="reds"/>  
</svg>  
One class applies the red #ll, and the other applies 50% opacity. !e rectangle in the middle belongs to both  
classes, so it’s both red and semi-transparent, as shown in Figure 3.9:  
Figure 3.9 – Applying CSS classes to SVG rectangles. Code: Shapes/SVG/6-rect-css-class.html  
Although HTML and SVG share many CSS properties and can share the same stylesheet, some properties,  
such as filland stroke, only exist in SVG. In HTML, background-colorand border-colorare used, which  
don’t work for SVG elements. !e only exception is the <svg> element itself, which is #lled and stroked  
with border-colorand background-color. But for any elements declared inside the <svg>element, such as  
<rect>, you need to use strokeand fill.  
Drawing paths  
11  
SVG shapes can be stroked and #lled with standard CSS color names (such as red, blue, or navy), hex  
codes (such as #ff0000 or #807be5), or color functions (rgb(100%, 50%, 25%), rgba(120,255,80,0.5),  
hsl(240,0.5,1), etc.) You can also #ll and stroke with patterns and gradients.  
Opacity  
In SVG, overlapping objects follow code order; elements declared later appear on top. !e CSS z-index  
property doesn’t apply to SVG. To bring an object to the front, you must modify the DOM tree.  
You can see through overlapping objects by adjusting their opacity with fill-opacity, stroke-opacity, or  
opacity (value between 0 and 1). An alternative is to set the alpha component in rgba or hsla CSS color  
functions. For example, the following CSS rule applies a 50% red #ll to all circles:  
circle { fill: rgba(255,0,0,0.5); }  
In the following example, the #rst two thin vertical rectangles appear behind a wide horizontal rectangle,  
and the last two appear in front of it:  
<rect x="300" y="50" height="150" width="25"/>  
<rect x="400" y="50" height="150" width="25" fill-opacity=".5"/>  
<rect x="250" y="100" height="50" width="300"  
<!-- Rectangle 1 -->  
<!-- Rectangle 3 -->  
fill="red" stroke="orange" stroke-width="10" stroke-opacity=".5" />  
<rect x="350" y="50" height="150" width="25"/>  
<rect x="450" y="50" height="150" width="25" fill-opacity=".5"/>  
<!-- Rectangle 2 -->  
<!-- Rectangle 4 -->  
Figure 3.10 shows the result of the preceding code. Note that the #rst and third thin rectangles from the le"  
were declared before the red rectangle and the second and fourth rectangles.  
Figure 3.10 – Rectangles with different fills, strokes, and transparency. Code: Shapes/SVG/7-rect-opacity.html  
Now that you know how to create basic shapes, you can use them as primitives and combine them to create  
complex drawings. In the next section, you will learn another way to create shapes.  
Drawing paths  
You can draw shapes that mix lines, curves, and arcs using a compact drawing language in the  
the <path>element. It can be used to create arbitrary paths:  
d
attribute of  
<path d="M100,200 C200,50 300,100 300,200 L400,250 500,100"  
fill="yellow" stroke="red" stroke-width="4"/>  
Essential SVG for D3  
12  
!e numbers and letters in the dattribute are directives that move (M) to the x,y position (100,200), draw a  
Bezier curve (C) with two control points at (200,50) and (300,100), ending in (300,200), and end with a line  
(L) to (400,250) and then to (500,100). In Figure 3.11, dots were added to show the control points:  
Figure 3.11 – A curve described by a <path> element. Code: Paths/SVG/1-path-line.html  
Path strings are typically generated by so"ware, but understanding them can be useful for debugging.  
Directives in Table 3.4 are followed by one or more values (separated by commas or spaces), which are  
absolute coordinates, for uppercase commands, or relative coordinates, for lowercase commands.  
Directive Coordinates Description  
M, m  
L, l  
V, v  
H, h  
C, c  
x y  
x y  
y
Move to. Moves to an x,y position.  
Line to. Draws a line to an x,y position.  
Vertical line. Draws a vertical line to a y position.  
Horizontal line. Draws a horizontal line to an x position.  
x
x1 y1 x2 y2  
x3 y3  
Bezier. Draws a Bezier curve from the current x,y position to x3,y3, using the control  
points x1,y1 and x2,y2, and ending in x3,y3.  
S, s  
x1 y1 x2 y2  
Smooth Bezier. Must follow a C/ccommand. Draws a Bezier using the second  
control point from the previous curve to infer the #rst control point, x1,y1 as the  
second, and ending in x2,y2. See an example in Paths/SVG/2-path-curve.html.  
Q, q  
x1 y1 x2 y2  
x1 y1  
Quadratic Bezier. Draws a Bezier with one control point x1,y1, ending in x2,y2.  
T, t  
Smooth Quadratic Bezier. Must follow a Q/qcommand. Draws a Bezier using the  
control point from the previous curve to infer the control point, ending in x1,y1.  
A, a  
rx ry rotx la  
sw x y  
Arc. Draws an arc with horizontal and vertical radii (rx,ry); the x-axis rotation angle  
in degrees rotx; two &ags, la (large-arc) and sw (sweep), which select one of the four  
possible arcs; and the #nal x,y position. See Paths/SVG/3-path-arc.html.  
Z, z  
None  
End. Closes a curve.  
Table 3.4 – Shape directives for the d attribute in <path>  
You will #nd some simple path examples containing lines, curves, and arcs in the Paths/ folder.  
Inserting text  
13  
Inserting text  
Unlike HTML, you can’t simply insert text in an SVG element. You need to create text objects with the <text>  
element and add the text to it. Text is positioned using  
x
and  
y
attributes, where  
y
is the baseline. If  
y
is  
0
(or  
not present), only the parts of the text that extend below the baseline are visible.  
!e following example places both text and a rectangle in the same position:  
<rect x="0" y="0" height="36" width="200"/>  
<text font-size="36" x="0" y="0" fill="lightgray">ghijklmnop</text>  
Figure 3.12 shows the result of di%erent alignment-baselinevalues applied to the <text>element, showing  
the e%ects of the default "baseline"(le"), "middle"(middle), and "hanging"(right).  
Figure 3.12 – Drawing text in SVG and the baseline. Code: Text/SVG/1-text.html  
Text can be aligned horizontally with text-anchor(Figure 3.13). When rotating text relative to its position,  
both values used in text-anchorand alignment-baselinewill a%ect the result.  
Figure 3.13 – Properties for aligning text. Code: Text/SVG/2-text-align.html  
Besides the xand yattributes, the text’s relative position can be controlled with dxand dy. !is makes it easier  
to place multiline text (but still not as easy as in HTML). In these cases, you can use <text>as a container  
for one or more <tspan>elements (see Text/SVG/3-tspan.html).  
Just like any other object, a text object can be moved, rotated, scaled, or skewed. It can also be rendered along  
a path with <textPath>. !e following code declares a path in a <defs>block (making it invisible), which  
is then referenced via its ID by <textPath>, which contains the text to be displayed:  
<defs>  
<path id="road" ... d="..." />  
</defs>  
<text text-anchor="middle">  
Essential SVG for D3  
14  
<textPath startOffset="50%" href="#road">  
This text is centered and uses the invisible path defined above as a baseline.  
</textPath>  
</text>  
!e result is shown in Figure 3.14.  
Figure 3.14 – Using <textPath> to draw text over a curved line. Code: Text/textPath.html  
!e startOffsetattribute determines where the text starts, relative to the path’s total length. Other attributes  
can also be used to distribute text along the path (see the documentation in the References section).  
Inserting images, links, and foreign objects  
!e <image> element allows a raster image to be imported into an SVG. It requires the same attributes as  
the <rect>element plus an hrefattribute containing the URL of the image. !e following code (Links/1-  
image.html) loads an image and displays it in the viewport:  
<image x="25" y="25" height="350" width="350" href="../../data/images/pluto.jpg"/>  
!e <a>element creates a link in SVG. It works like the HTML <a>and requires an hrefattribute with the  
URL of the linked resource. !e element’s body should contain the clickable elements that trigger the link.  
!e following code will download an image #le when the user clicks on the text and place it in a browser  
window or frame named 'result':  
<a href="../../data/flags/CHN.jpg" target="result"><text>...</text></a>  
<foreignObject>is an extension point to include code from HTML or other XML applications. See Links/2-  
foreign-object.htmlfor an example that embeds an <iframe>in SVG.  
Before exploring more elements, let’s learn how to move, scale, and rotate elements with transforms. !is is  
a rather complex topic, but with simple examples, we can make the learning curve smoother.  
Affine transforms  
!e initial viewport establishes a default coordinate system used by all child elements, where each unit in  
any direction corresponds to one pixel and coordinates start at the top-le" corner. !is coordinate system  
can be modi#ed in two ways: setting the viewBoxattribute or applying a$ne transforms.  
A$ne transforms  
15  
!e transformXML attribute (also a CSS property) applies a$ne transforms (matrix multiplication) to any  
shape or group of shapes. !e operation is set up by assigning values to the six components that form the  
#rst two rows of the transformation matrix, which is the identity matrix by default (no transformation).  
!is can be done with the matrix()function (Figure 3.15).  
Figure 3.15 – The identity matrix and the matrix used for affine transforms. The a, c, b, and d components  
represent linear transformations (rotation, scaling, shearing). The e and f components represent translation  
Note that the parameters are ordered by column, not by row. !e default component values, which correspond  
to no transformation, are  
a
,
d
= 1, and  
b
,
c
,
e
,
f
= 0. For example, if you apply the following transform to a  
circle, nothing happens, since no components were changed:  
<circle cx="25" cy="25" r="50" transform="matrix(1,0,0,1,0,0)"/>  
!e following transform changes the last two matrix components (the third column), which performs a  
translation operation. !is moves the circle 75 pixels le" and 50 pixels down:  
<circle cx="25" cy="25" r="50" transform="matrix(1,0,0,1,75,50)"/>  
!is operation e%ectively changes the coordinate system used by the circle. !e position of its center (cx  
and cy) is now relative to the new local coordinate system created by the transform. !e circle’s absolute  
position (relative to its container) has changed to (100,75), which is the sum of its center coordinates and  
corresponding translation parameters (Figure 3.16).  
Figure 3.16 – Before and after applying a coordinate transform on a circle. Code: Transforms/1-translate.html  
Essential SVG for D3  
16  
Of course, in this simple example, you could obtain the same visual result by simply changing the circle’s cx  
and cyattributes, but transforms are much more powerful, as we will see.  
You can use matrix()to perform any kind of transformation, but it’s usually simpler to call speci#c operations  
that just change the relevant components, especially when it involves angles, such as shearing or rotation.  
!e same transformation as shown previously could be performed with the following:  
<circle cx="25" cy="25" r="50" transform="translate(75,50)"/>  
Table 3.5 lists and describes all the operations you can perform using the transformattribute.  
Operation  
Description  
translate(x,y)  
Moves to x,y. Same as matrix(1,0,0,1,x,y). Translation along the x axis requires a  
single argument: translate(x) is the same as translate(x,0).  
scale(h,v)  
Scales by a factor of h (x axis) and v (y axis). Same as matrix(h,0,0,v,0,0). To scale  
the same factor on both axes, provide a single argument: scale(z) is the same as  
scale(z,z). Negative values &ip about the opposite axis.  
rotate(a,x,y)  
Rotates by a degrees at x,y. Equivalent to matrix(cos a,-sin a,sin a,cos a,x,y). !e  
last two arguments are optional: rotate(a) is rotate(a,0,0). !e transform-origin  
attribute (or CSS property) can also be used to set the center of rotation.  
skewX(a)  
Shears horizontally by a degrees. Same as matrix(1,0,tan a,1,0,0).  
skewY(a)  
Shears vertically by a degrees. Same as matrix(1,tan a,0,1,0,0).  
matrix(a,b,c,d,e,f)  
All cumulative transforms can be applied with a single matrix()command. Figure 3.17  
shows how the preceding basic transforms are related to matrix().  
Table 3.5 – SVG coordinate transforms (used in the transform attribute)  
Although it’s usually more convenient to apply simple transforms using translate(), scale(), and rotate(),  
it’s useful to know how these functions are related to the matrix()syntax (Figure 3.17).  
A$ne transforms  
17  
Figure 3.17 – Affine transformations, the functions used to apply them in SVG, and affected matrix components  
Quick tip: Need to see a high-resolution version of this image? Open this book in the next-gen  
Packt Reader or view it in the PDF/ePub copy.  
!e next-gen Packt Reader and a free PDF/ePub copy of this book are included with your purchase.  
Scan the QR code OR go to packtpub.com/unlock, then use the search bar to #nd this book by name.  
Double-check the edition shown to make sure you get the right one.  
!e current transformation matrix (CTM) contains the cumulative transforms applied to an object.  
Using the DOM, you can obtain an object’s CTM and extract the six components required by the matrix()  
operation. We will explore the CTM in the Using the DOM API section.  
Essential SVG for D3  
18  
Cumulative transforms  
A single transform can perform a sequence of cumulative operations. For example, the following code will  
translate and then scale the circle, displaying it in half its original size:  
<circle cx="25" cy="25" r="50" transform="translate(75,50) scale(0.5)"/>  
Note that each operation runs in a coordinate system created by the preceding operation. !is means that  
if you scale #rst, the translation will occur in the new scaled coordinate system where one unit corresponds  
to a half-pixel, placing the circle in di%erent absolute coordinates (Figure 3.18).  
Figure 3.18 – The order of transform operations affects the results. Code: Transforms/2-cumulative.html  
Note that the matrices, which represent the cumulative transforms, are di%erent. To achieve the same e%ect,  
you would need to double the x and y parameters for the translate()operation:  
<circle cx="25" cy="25" r="50" transform="scale(0.5) translate(150,100)"/>  
You can compare these results in Transforms/2-cumulative.html.  
Transforms in CSS  
!e same transforms applied with the transformXML attribute can be applied in CSS, but since CSS doesn’t  
use default units, you must append the unit a"er the values (e.g., 'px', 'deg', etc.)  
For example, consider the following transform using the XML attribute:  
<rect ... transform="rotate(-30,50,25) translate(10,15)" />  
A$ne transforms  
19  
In CSS, you can apply it in a stylesheet (see Transforms/3-transform-css.html), but it must use units:  
rect {  
transform-origin: 50px 25px;  
transform: rotate(-30deg) translate(10px,15px);  
}
!ere are also some di%erences. XML attributes only support the functions listed in Table 3.5 (CSS supports  
more), and the rotate() function in CSS takes a single argument (to set the center, you need to set the  
transform-originproperty, as shown previously).  
Flipping an object  
Calling scale()with negative numbers will re&ect the object’s coordinate system about the origin or an axis.  
!e following transform &ips the polygon about the origin without changing its size:  
<polygon points="0,0 100,5 75,25 100,45 0,50" transform="scale(-1)"/>  
Calling scale() with two arguments applies scaling to the x and y axes separately. If the #rst argument is  
positive and the second is negative, the transform will &ip the object vertically, about the x axis. Inverting  
the signs &ips the object horizontally, about the y axis:  
<polygon points="0,0 100,5 75,25 100,45 0,50" transform="scale(-1,1)" />  
If your origin is located at the default top le", all these transforms move the object o%screen. To &ip an object  
about its center, you must change its transformation origin. !e best way to do this is to set transform-origin  
with the object’s center coordinates:  
<polygon points="..." transform-origin="50 25" transform="scale(-1,1)" />  
!ese transforms are shown in Figure 3.19. !e circle indicates the origin used for each transform.  
Figure 3.19 – Using scale() to flip an object. Code: Transform/4-scale-flip.html  
Essential SVG for D3  
20  
!e transform-originattribute (also a CSS property) is particularly useful in operations that involve angles,  
such as rotating and shearing.  
Rotating and shearing  
A matrix()operation for rotating or shearing requires computing sines and cosines, or tangents of angles  
in radians. It’s more convenient to use rotate(), skewX(), and skewY(), which take angles in degrees.  
!e rotation origin is provided by the shape’s parent container, the previous transform, or the transform-  
origin property. Setting the correct origin is critical to avoid rotating a shape o%screen. !e following  
polygon, for example, is rotated -45 degrees. It would vanish if rotated -90 degrees:  
<polygon points="0,0 100,5 75,25 100,45 0,50" transform="rotate(-45)"/>  
To rotate it around its center, either use rotate()with three arguments or use transform-origin:  
<polygon points="..." transform-origin="50 25" transform="rotate(-45)"/>  
Figure 3.20 shows the e%ect of rotating about the origin and about the center of the shape.  
Figure 3.20 – Using rotate() to rotate an object 45 degrees counterclockwise. Code: Transform/5-rotate-skew.html  
You will #nd more examples in the Transform/folder, which also contains examples using shearing.  
Group containers  
Groups are lightweight containers that wrap SVG elements in a <g> block. !ey don’t have height, width,  
x, or yattributes. To move a group, you need to apply a transform:  
<svg width="900" height="300">  
<g>  
<circle cx="150" cy="150" r="120" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>  
<rect x="30" y="140" height="20" width="240" fill="currentColor"/>  
<rect x="140" y="30" width="20" height="240" fill="currentColor"/>  
</g>  
<g color="red" transform="translate(300,0)">  
<circle cx="150" cy="150" r="120" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>  
<rect x="30" y="140" height="20" width="240" fill="currentColor"/>  
Reusable objects  
21  
<rect x="140" y="30" width="20" height="240" fill="currentColor"/>  
</g>  
</svg>  
Groups can’t be #lled or stroked but a <g>with strokeand fillattributes applies these styles to descendants  
that don’t de#ne these attributes.  
If the elements in a group set their fillor strokevalue to currentColor, you can set the colorattribute in  
a group to apply the same color to #ll and stroke its descendants, as shown previously.  
!e preceding code produces the image shown in Figure 3.21.  
Figure 3.21 – Applying a color to strokes and fills using currentColor. Code: Containers/SVG/2-groups.html  
Applying a transform to a group creates a new coordinate system. !e attributes of the shapes in the second  
group are relative to this new system. !e shapes in both <g>blocks have the same coordinates.  
Reusable objects  
!e <defs>element stores de#nitions for reusable structures. Shapes or groups declared in it are not rendered.  
!ey can, however, be referenced outside the <defs>block and be used multiple times.  
Instead of duplicating code to draw a new symbol, here we declare the same group inside a <defs> block,  
with an id="earth"attribute. It won’t appear on the screen, but it can be reused:  
<defs>  
<g id="earth">  
<circle cx="150" cy="150" r="120" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>  
<rect x="30" y="140" height="20" width="240" fill="currentColor"/>  
<rect y="30" x="140" width="20" height="240" fill="currentColor"/>  
</g>  
</defs>  
!e <use/>element reuses any object that has an id. In the following example, it reused the #earthgroup  
three times, applying di%erent colors and transforms in each case:  
<use href="#earth" />  
<use href="#earth" color="red" transform="scale(0.5) translate(600,0) "/>  
<use href="#earth" color="blue" transform="translate(450,0) rotate(45,150,150)"/>  
Essential SVG for D3  
22  
!e result is shown in Figure 3.22.  
Figure 3.22 – Reusing symbols declared in a <defs> block with <use>. Code: Containers/SVG/3-defs-use.html  
Objects can also be reused using <svg>in <defs>(see Containers/SVG/5-svg-reuse.html). Unlike <g>, an  
<svg>can have widthand heightand can set a new coordinate system with the viewBoxattribute.  
viewBox  
!e optional viewBoxattribute, supported by <svg>and a few other containers, sets a rectangular viewport  
with local units. It contains four numbers separated by spaces. !e top-le" corner, which defaults to 0 0,  
and width and height, which default to the declared or default pixel height and width of the container. !is  
attribute can move the viewport to a di%erent position and change its local units.  
For example, a viewBoxdeclared as 0 0 400 300in a 400x300-pixel SVG changes nothing:  
<svg width="400" height="300" viewBox="0 0 400 300">  
<rect width="350" height="250"/>  
<circle r="100"/>  
<line y2="90"/>  
<line x2="90"/>  
</svg>  
!is SVG draws a rectangle, a circle, and two lines at 0,0. Only the bottom-right quadrant is visible since  
the origin coincides with the top-le" corner of the viewport (Figure 3.23).  
!e circle has a radius of 100 units, rendered as 100 pixels. Since a circle’s default origin is its center, only  
the bottom-right quarter of the circle is visible. !e two lines start at the origin (0,0), which is also where  
the center of the circle is placed. One moves 90 pixels down, while the other moves 90 pixels right. Only half  
of their stroke is visible. A rectangle’s default origin is its top-le" corner. Since the rectangle is smaller than  
the viewport, it’s fully visible. Only its top and le" strokes are thinner, since half their width is o%screen.  
Figure 3.23 – A circle at the origin in a default viewport. Code: Containers/SVG/6-viewBox-default.html  
Other SVG elements  
23  
You can use viewBoxto move the top-le" corner. For example, viewBox="-200 -150 400 300"will place the  
origin at the center of the viewport, by moving the top-le" corner halfway up and le". Now, with the origin  
at the center, you can see the entire circle and full stroke widths (Figure 3.24).  
Figure 3.24 – Moving the origin to the center of the viewport. Code: Containers/SVG/7-viewBox-center.html  
viewBoxcan also be used to scale the coordinate system. With viewBox="-400 -300 800 600", it now contains  
twice as many units per pixels, while still at the center. !is will display all the objects at half their original  
size, since the 400-pixel-wide SVG now #ts 800 units. !is means that a circle with a radius of 100 units will  
be rendered with a 50-pixel radius (Figure 3.25).  
Figure 3.25 – In this viewport, each pixel corresponds to two units. Code: Containers/SVG/8-viewBox-scaled.html  
You can scale the contents of the container by changing its widthand heightattributes to #t the viewport  
wherever you want. Using percentages for height and width #ts it in its parent container (e.g., a <div>),  
allowing for responsive graphics. If width and height are not proportional to viewBox dimensions, the  
contents are scaled, preserving the aspect ratio. !is can be changed with preserveAspectRatio="none".  
We have now covered all SVG tags and attributes you will most likely use with D3. !e remaining sections  
will explore a few other tags and attributes that you might not use as frequently.  
Other SVG elements  
!is section covers SVG elements for reusable symbols and markers, gradients, clippings, masks, #lters,  
and animation. Most provide functionality that may be easier to implement in CSS (e.g., #lters, masks, and  
gradients) or D3 (e.g., animations). In some cases, however, they may be a simpler alternative.  
Essential SVG for D3  
24  
Symbols  
Reusable shapes can be created with <symbol>. !ey are more &exible than using <g>for this purpose since  
they can be con#gured with viewBox:  
<svg width="460" height="300" >  
<defs>  
<symbol viewBox="-15 -15 30 30" width="30" height="30" id="earth">  
<circle r="12" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"></circle>  
<rect x="-12" y="-1" height="2" width="24" fill="currentColor"/>  
<rect y="-12" x="-1" width="2" height="24" fill="currentColor"/>  
</symbol>  
</defs>  
<use href="#earth"/>  
<use href="#earth" transform="scale(10)" color="green"/>  
<use href="#earth" transform="translate(300,75) scale(5)" color="orange"/>  
</svg>  
!e preceding code produces the result shown in Figure 3.26.  
Figure 3.26 – A symbol created with a nested <svg> or <symbol> container. Code: Other/SVG/3-symbols.html  
!e same result is obtained with a nested <svg>(see Containers/SVG/5-svg-reuse.html), but symbols are  
more e$cient, as they are only created when referenced by a <use/>element, if necessary.  
Markers  
A marker is a reusable symbol that can be attached to line ends and vertices. It is commonly used for arrowheads  
or to highlight points in a shape. To create one, declare the <marker> tag in a <defs> block, determine its  
user space (viewBox), and add shapes that #t in that space. To use a marker, you need to link it to a shape.  
!e size of the marker will change proportionally to the shape’s stroke-width property. Figure 3.27 shows  
an example of a marker created in a 10x6 space.  
Other SVG elements  
25  
Figure 3.27 – A reusable SVG marker. Code: Other/SVG/4-marker-template.html  
!e following code de#nes the marker represented in Figure 3.27. !e orient="auto"attribute guarantees  
that it will always be parallel to the line, attached at refYand refX:  
<defs>  
<marker id="arrow" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="6"  
refY="3" refX="2" orient="auto">  
<path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="red"/>  
</marker>  
</defs>  
A marker can be attached to a shape using marker-mid, to apply it to all vertices; marker-endto apply it to  
the end of a line; or marker-start, to apply it to the beginning of the line. !e same marker can be reused at  
the beginning, rotated 180 degrees if orientis de#ned as auto-start-reverse. Note that the link requires  
url(#id)(instead of simply #id, used in hrefattributes):  
<path d="..." fill="none" ="red" stroke-width="4" marker-end="url(#arrow)" .../>  
!e line in Figure 3.28 applies the marker to a path using orient=auto-start-reverse. It also applies  
composite markers to the vertices. See the complete code in Other/SVG/5-marker-html.  
Figure 3.28 – Using markers applied to a path. Code: Other/SVG/5-marker.html  
It’s usually simpler to apply clipping, masks, and #lters via CSS, when using SVG in HTML, but a library such  
as D3 can give you more control over these e%ects when implemented in SVG. Let’s see how to use them.  
Essential SVG for D3  
26  
Clipping paths  
A clipping path allows you to clip o% parts of any graphics. It is typically used with images. To create one,  
add a <clipPath> element to the <defs> header containing or referencing the shape that will provide the  
mask and declare an idvalue so it can be referenced by the object to be clipped:  
<defs>  
<clipPath id="clip">  
<circle r="139" cx="200" cy="199"/>  
</clipPath>  
</defs>  
To use a clipping path, reference it with url(#id)inside a clip-pathattribute:  
<image x="25" y="25" height="350" width="350" href="../../data/images/pluto.jpg"  
clip-path=»url(#clip)»/>  
!e preceding code de#nes a circle as the clipping mask. When composed with an image, only the parts of  
the image that #t inside the circle will be preserved. !e rest will be cropped. Figure 3.29 shows the original  
image (le") and the clipped image (right).  
Figure 3.29 – Clipping or masking an image in SVG. Code: Other/SVG/6-clipping.html or 7-mask.html  
Clipping is great if you need to apply an all-or-nothing e%ect on an image. If instead you wish to fade parts  
of the image or apply a grayscale #lter, you can use <mask>.  
Masks  
!e <mask>element uses the luminance of a color as a level of opacity when applied to an object. !is a%ects  
how the object interacts with anything behind it. White is opaque, and black is transparent. !e following  
code uses <mask>for the same e%ect as <clipPath>(see Other/SVG/7-mask.html):  
<defs>  
<mask id="mask">  
<rect x="25" y="25" height="350" width="350" fill="white"/>  
<!-- Opaque -->  
Other SVG elements  
27  
<circle r="139" cx="200" cy="199" fill="black"/>  
</mask>  
</defs>  
<!-- Transparent -->  
Here, the mask is applied to a white square placed on the image. It will cut open a transparent circle on the  
square, revealing the image behind it:  
<image x="25" y="25" height="350" width="350" href="../../data/images/pluto.jpg"/>  
<rect x="25" y="25" height="350" width="350" fill="white" mask="url(#mask)" />  
!e result should be identical to Figure 3.29. But with masks, you can apply tones of gray to create semitransparent  
masks. If you use colors, their luminance is applied to the #lter. Images can be used to con#gure the mask  
(see Other/SVG/8-image-mask.html), but to compose in color, you need a #lter.  
Filters  
SVG #lters can be applied to shapes, text, or images. !ey are usually created in the <defs> header and  
referenced via url(#id using the filter attribute. !e <filter>element creates a #lter by con#guring a  
)
#lter type (elements that start in <fe*>). You can create composite #lters by adding more #lter types.  
!is <defs>block de#nes two con#gurations for the <feGaussianBlur>#lter:  
<defs>  
<filter id="filter1">  
<feGaussianBlur stdDeviation="7" />  
</filter>  
<filter id="filter2" x="-100" y="-100" height="200" width="200">  
<feGaussianBlur stdDeviation="0,5" in="SourceGraphic" />  
</filter>  
</defs>  
To use the #lters, reference them from a shape via the filterattribute, as shown in the following code block.  
!is code will apply the second #lter to the text, and the #rst one to the blue circle:  
<text id="text" x="50" y="60" filter="url(#filter2)">Do you need glasses?</text>  
<g id="stardot" transform="translate(100,25) scale(0.5)">  
<polygon points="250,0 400,500 0,200 500,200 100,500"  
fill="red" fill-rule="evenodd"/>  
<circle cx="250" cy="283" r="75" fill="blue" filter="url(#filter1)" />  
</g>  
Essential SVG for D3  
28  
!e result is shown in Figure 3.30.  
Figure 3.30 – A Gaussian blur filter applied to different shapes. Code: Other/SVG/9-filter.html  
!ese are just a few of the many #lters and e%ects that you can apply to your shapes using SVG.  
Gradients  
SVG supports two types of gradients: linear and radial. Linear gradients are perpendicular to a line and  
require the same attributes as the <line>element. Radial gradients use <circle>attributes.  
Gradients typically declare two or more child <stop>colors, which con#gure each color with an offsetand  
stop-color. !e following <defs>block declares two gradients:  
<defs>  
<linearGradient x2="1" id="rainbow">  
<stop offset="0"  
stop-color="rgb(255,0,0)" />  
<stop offset="0.25" stop-color="rgb(255,255,64)" />  
<stop offset="0.5" stop-color="rgb(64,255,64)" />  
<stop offset="0.75" stop-color="rgb(64,64,255)" />  
<stop offset="1"  
</linearGradient>  
stop-color="rgb(128,0,255)" />  
<radialGradient cx="0.35" cy="0.35" r="1" id="glow">  
<stop offset="0" stop-color="rgb(255,255,255)" />  
<stop offset="0.5" stop-color="rgb(0,128,255)" />  
<stop offset="1"  
</radialGradient>  
stop-color="rgb(128,0,255)" />  
</defs>  
A"er you declare it, you can use a gradient as a color and reference it in the filland strokeattributes via  
url(#id). Here, they are used to stroke and #ll three shapes:  
<rect width="300" height="50" fill="url(#rainbow)" transform="translate(50,25)"/>  
<rect width="300" height="50" stroke="url(#rainbow)" fill="none" stroke-width="10"  
transform="rotate(180,200,145) translate(50,120)"/>  
<circle r="75" fill="url(#glow)" transform="translate(475,100)"/>  
Other SVG elements  
29  
!e results are shown in Figure 3.31.  
Figure 3.31 – Gradients applied to rectangles (linear) and a circle (radial). Code: Other/SVG/10-gradient.html  
You can use gradients to display di%erent colors in a heatmap.  
Patterns  
Repeatable patterns can be created with the <pattern> element, which provides a local coordinate system  
for the shapes that will repeat. !e following code creates a simple crosshatch pattern:  
<pattern id="crosshatch" viewBox="0 0 10 10" width="10" height="10"  
patternUnits="userSpaceOnUse">  
<path d="M0,0 L0,2 8,10 10,10 z"/>  
<path d="M8,0 L10,0 10,2 z"/>  
<path d="M0,0 L0,2 8,10 10,10 z" transform="rotate(90,5,5)"/>  
<path d="M8,0 L10,0 10,2 z" transform="rotate(90,5,5)"/>  
</pattern>  
!e patternUnitsattribute is used to preserve the size of the shapes when they are rendered. Once declared,  
the pattern can be used as a #ll or stroke color. Here, it’s used to paint a rectangle:  
<rect height="50" width="100" fill="url(#crosshatch)" transform="translate(50,120)" />  
!e preceding code was used to paint the third rectangle in Figure 3.32.  
Figure 3.32 – A pattern used as a fill color and as a stroke color. Code: Other/SVG/11-pattern.html  
!e preceding patterns were created in black, but they can be any color. !ey can even include images.  
Patterns are a great alternative to paint charts with textures when you can’t use colors.  
Essential SVG for D3  
30  
Animation elements  
SVG supports animation using the <animate>, <animateMotion>, <animateTransform>, and <set>tags. Much  
of this functionality is duplicated in other widely available APIs, such as CSS animations and transitions. D3  
also provides its own API for animated transitions, which is easier to use for data visualizations.  
Some animations may be simpler to implement in SVG, such as moving an object along a path. We will  
explore them in Chapter 13. See some animations created with these tags in the Animation/folder.  
!e #nal section in this chapter explores advanced concepts that involve JavaScript programming and the  
SVG DOM. If you wish, you can skip it and refer to it later when you need it.  
Using the DOM API  
SVG elements can be selected using DOM methods such as getElementById()or querySelector(). While  
D3 eliminates most of the work in selecting and transforming elements, you might still need to perform  
operations on SVG elements. Table 3.6 lists some useful methods you can use.  
Method  
DOM Interface  
Description  
getCTM()  
SVGGraphicsElement  
SVGGraphicsElement  
Returns the CTM (an SVGMatrixobject).  
getScreenCTM()  
Returns the CTM (SVGMatrix) for a graphics  
element in screen coordinates (relative to page, not  
SVG viewport).  
getTotalLength()  
getPointAtLength(dist)  
isPointInFill  
SVGGeometryElement  
SVGGeometryElement  
SVGGeometryElement  
SVGGeometryElement  
Returns the total length of a path, line, polyline, or  
other geometry element.  
Returns an SVGPointat a certain distance from the  
start of a path.  
Returns true if a point (must be an SVGPoint  
object) is in the #ll area of an object.  
isPointInStroke  
Returns true if a point (must be an SVGPoint  
object) is in the stroke area of an object.  
getComputedTextLength()  
getBBox  
SVGTextContentElement  
SVGGraphicsElement  
Returns the length of rendered text.  
Returns the bounding rectangle of a shape (an  
SVGRect). Does not consider any transforms  
applied to the shape. Coordinates are relative to the  
SVG viewport.  
getBoundingClientRect()  
elementsFromPoint()  
Element  
Gets the bounding rectangle of a shape (a DOMRect).  
Coordinates are relative to the page.  
Document  
Returns an array of elements that contain a  
given point.  
Table 3.6 – Some useful DOM methods for SVG graphics  
Using the DOM API  
31  
!e methods in Table 3.6 return three types of DOM interfaces: rectangles (DOMRect and SVGRect), with  
, height, and width properties; points (DOMPointand SVGPoint), with and properties; and matrices  
, and properties. Although they are considered aliases, they  
x
,
y
x
y
(DOMMatrix and SVGMatrix), with  
a
,
b
,
c
,
d
,
e
f
unfortunately can’t yet be used interchangeably in all browsers. But DOM objects can be created from SVG  
objects with DOMPoint.fromPoint(), DOMRect.fromRect(), and DOMMatrix.fromMatrix().  
Methods de#ned in SVG interfaces usually provide data relative to the SVG container, while other DOM  
interfaces based on the page or screen need to be adjusted to match the viewport’s coordinate system.  
Let’s see some examples using these methods.  
Using the current transformation matrix  
!e CTM stores information about cumulative transforms applied to an object in the coordinate system of  
its parent container. Calling getCTM() on a shape or group returns an SVGMatrix from where you can get  
its components (  
a
,
b
,
c
,
d
,
e
, and  
f
properties) and use them to create new transforms or apply an inverse  
transform on another object. For example, consider the following <rect>:  
<rect ... transform="translate(45,80) rotate(-40) scale(1.5)" />  
!e following code (API/SVG/1-ctm.html) will obtain its CTM:  
const rect = document.querySelector("rect");  
const ctm = rect.getCTM();  
// This is an SVGMatrix  
!en use the CTM to apply the same transform to other objects, for example, to an existing path:  
path.setAttribute("transform", `matrix(${ctm.a,ctm.b,ctm.c,ctm.d,ctm.e,ctm.f})`);  
!e DOMMatrixinterface has a toString()method that generates the matrix()string required by transform.  
To use it, you must #rst convert the SVGMatrixinto a DOMMatrix, as shown here:  
const domMatrix = DOMMatrix.fromMatrix(ctm);  
path.setAttribute("transform", domMatrix.toString());  
!e CTM contains methods, such as inverse(), which inverts the matrix. !is is useful to obtain coordinates  
before a transformation (or viewBoxcon#guration) or to simply revert a transform:  
const itm = ctm.inverse();  
Here, the inverse was used to undo a transform applied to a rectangle (API/SVG/2-ctm-inverse.html):  
const inv = DOMMatrix.fromMatrix(itm);  
// convert to DOMMatrix  
rect.setAttribute("transform", inv.toString());  
// generate matrix(a,b,c,d,e,f)  
Essential SVG for D3  
32  
If you are working with operations that depend on the screen’s coordinate system (e.g., mouse events), you  
can call getScreenCTM()to get the CTM relative to the page. For example, the eventobject received by an  
event handler contains  
x
and  
y
in screen coordinates. !e following code uses the inverse screen CTM to  
convert them to local SVG coordinates so that the circle is placed in the correct position:  
svg.addEventListener("click", event => {  
const stm = svg.getScreenCTM();  
const pt = DOMPoint.fromPoint(event).matrixTransform(stm.inverse());  
circle.setAttribute("cx", pt.x);  
// corrected coordinates  
circle.setAttribute("cy", pt.y);  
}
!e fromPoint()method accepts any object that has  
x
and  
y
properties (such as event), and matrixTransform()  
applies a matrix transform to the point (in this case, the inverse screen CTM).  
Correcting individual points only #xes the o%set. If your container has a viewBox or transforms such as  
scaling or rotation, you may need to apply the entire inverse transform to the circle itself:  
const itm = DOMMatrix.fromMatrix(stm.inverse());  
circle.setAttribute("transform", itm.toString());  
// generates matrix(a,b,c,d,e,f)  
See an interactive example using this code in API/SVG/3-screen-ctm.html.  
Getting lengths and bounds  
To compute the total length of a line or path or the perimeter of any shape, call getTotalLength(). For  
example, this will return the circumference of a circle (see API/SVG/4-lengths.html):  
const circle = document.querySelector("circle");  
// any <circle>  
console.log(`Circumference: ${circle.getTotalLength()} pixels`);  
To #t a string of text in a rectangle, you must know its length a"er the string is rendered. You can get it by  
calling getComputedTextLength()on a <text>or <tspan>object:  
const text = document.querySelector("text");  
// <text>Lorem ipsum</text>  
console.log(`Computed text length: ${text.getComputedTextLength()} pixels`);  
If the text is rotated, has a curved baseline, or you want to know its center, you need its bounding box: a  
DOMRector SVGRectthat describes the smallest rectangle containing a shape or a group of shapes. Figure 3.33  
shows the bounding boxes of several shapes and groups:  
Figure 3.33 – Bounding boxes. Code: API/SVG/5-bounding-client-rect.html or 6-bbox.html  
Using the DOM API  
33  
!ere are two methods you can use to obtain a bounding box. Although they return di%erent interfaces,  
they support the same  
x
,
y, height, and widthproperties that draw a rectangle. Both, however, may require  
some corrections. For example, consider the circle in Figure 3.33 de#ned by the following SVG:  
<circle r="50" transform="translate(500,100)" fill="green"/>  
Calling getBoundingClientRect()returns a DOMRectobject. Since it is not an SVG object,  
x
and  
y
are returned  
in screen coordinates and need to be converted to local coordinates, applying the inverse CTM transform  
to the points, as shown here:  
const box = circle.getBoundingClientRect(); // {height:100, width:100, x:459, y:287}  
const stm = svg.getScreenCTM();  
// {a: 1, b: 0, c: 0, d: 1, e: 9, f: 237}  
const pt = DOMPoint.fromPoint(box).matrixTransform(stm.inverse());  
box.x = pt.x;  
box.y = pt.y;  
// x: 450  
// y: 50  
Since getBBoxis SVG-speci#c, it returns an SVGRectobject in local coordinates, but unfortunately, it doesn’t  
consider transforms. !e bounding box for the circle treats it as if it were at the origin:  
const box = circle.getBBox();  
// {height: 100, width: 100, x: -50, y: -50}  
!e transform is a simple translation, which can be #xed using the circle’s local CTM:  
const ctm = circle.getCTM();  
box.x = ctm.e;  
box.y = ctm.f;  
// {a: 1, b: 0, c: 0, d: 1, e: 500, f: 100}  
// x: 450  
// y: 50  
But this will be much harder if you have scaled units (viewBox), rotations, and cumulative transforms. For  
example, the rectangle in Figure 3.33 is rotated 45 degrees:  
<rect x="50" y="50" width="100" height="100" fill="red"  
transform-origin="100 100" transform="rotate(45)"/>  
You can’t rotate a bounding box. A solution is to place the rotated rectangle inside a <g>container, translate  
the container (if necessary), and get the container’s bounding box. In such cases, it’s probably simpler to use  
getBoundingClientRect()and compensate the screen o%set.  
Finding points  
Calling getPointAtLength(distance)on a path returns the point at the speci#ed position. !is method can  
be used to place an object on the border of a shape. If you animate the distance, you can use it to make an  
object move along a path (see API/SVG/7-point-at-length.html).  
Although getPointAtLength() is an SVG method, it doesn’t consider transforms. Consider the following  
setup, with a scaling viewBoxapplied to the viewport, and a vertical scale applied to the path:  
<svg width="1000" height="500" viewBox="0 0 500 250">  
<path d="..." ... transform="scale(1,.5)"/>  
</svg>  
Essential SVG for D3  
34  
To obtain the correct coordinates for the points, you need to invert their viewBoxtransforms, and then apply  
the path transforms, as follows (see API/SVG/8-point-transforms.html):  
const pt = path.getPointAtLength(distance); // point not considering transforms  
const spt = pt.matrixTransform(svg.getCTM().inverse()); // invert SVG viewBox  
const point = spt.matrixTransform(path.getCTM());  
// apply path transform  
!e best way to discover whether a point is in a shape is with elementsFromPoint(), a DOM method that  
returns an array with all elements containing the point. It uses screen coordinates (it’s not an SVG method).  
For example, this prints all elements that contain a clicked point (API/SVG/9-elements-from-point.html):  
svg.addEventListener("click", function(e) {  
const pt = DOMPoint.fromPoint(e);  
const elements = document.elementsFromPoint(pt.x, pt.y);  
console.log(elements);  
});  
You can use isPointInFill() and isPointInStroke() to test whether a point is in a shape’s #ll or stroke  
(see API/SVG/10-in-fill-stroke.html). !ey work in simple scenarios but may require complex code if  
you must consider transforms and viewBox. Using elementsFromPoint()is usually simpler in these cases.  
!ese methods are used in API/SVG/11-locating-points.html(Figure 3.34), where the color of each circle  
is assigned based on its position in relation to the shape. You can also click anywhere on the SVG to draw  
a square of the corresponding color.  
Figure 3.34 – Discovering whether a point is inside a shape or on its border. Code: API/SVG/11-locating-points.html  
!is section covered several advanced concepts using the SVG DOM. !e code is in the API folder. !e  
SVG/subfolder contains examples in pure JavaScript, while the D3/subfolder implements them using D3.  
Before moving on to the next chapter, open a web page and try out some examples from this chapter; create  
shapes and apply #lls, stroke e%ects, and #lters. Try drawing some polygons and simple paths and experiment  
with applying transforms to groups of elements. !is will make your D3 journey much smoother.  
Summary  
35  
Summary  
!is chapter provided a detailed overview covering essential topics in the SVG technology, the main open  
standard that D3 uses to draw graphics. Advanced topics, such as matrix transforms and DOM methods,  
were also covered.  
Although this chapter explored the most important SVG elements and attributes, many other features were  
le" out. See the References section for links to specs and documentation about SVG.  
In the next chapter, you will #nally dive into the world of D3 and create a complete interactive animated bar  
chart using SVG shapes, exploring a little of the features we will cover in detail later in this book.  
References  
1. "e o#cial SVG 1.1 speci!cation. All modern browsers support it: www.w3.org/TR/SVG11/.  
2. "e SVG 2 speci!cation. Although not fully supported, many individual features are supported by  
modern browsers and are recommended: www.w3.org/TR/SVG2/.  
3. MDN SVG reference and tutorial: developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/SVG/.
